items.title: Spotlight: Test Your Robot’s Skills in NIST’s Global Online Competition
items.content_text: How can researchers know how skilled a robot is at simple tasks, such as picking things up and putting them down?

items.title: Department of Commerce Announces Definitive Agreement with SandboxAQ for a $500 Million CHIPS R&D Award to Accelerate Al-Driven Semiconductor Materials Discovery
items.content_text: The Department of Commerce’s CHIPS Research & Development Office today announced the signing of a definitive agreement with SandboxAQ for a $500 million award under the CHIPS and Science Act.

items.title: The Department of Commerce’s CHIPS Program Announces a Letter of Intent with Coherent for up to $50 Million to Expand Indium Phosphide Production
items.content_text: The Department of Commerce’s CHIPS Program Office announced the signing of a letter of intent to provide up to $50 million in direct funding to Coherent Corp. (Coherent) under the CHIPS and Science Act.

items.title: Apollo Research is becoming a PBC – Apollo Research
items.content_text: Apollo is spinning off from our fiscal sponsor into a Public Benefit Corporation (PBC). We think this is the best way for us to achieve our mission of reducing extreme risks from frontier AI systems.

items.title: Our Norms on Security, Science Communication and Conflicts of Interest – Apollo Research
items.content_text: We outline Apollo Research’s norms on security, science communication, and conflicts of interest, detailing how we maintain scientific integrity, manage sensitive information, and communicate our work responsibly.

items.title: CANCELED - 2070 Balance and Scale Calibration and Uncertainties
items.content_text: This 4-day seminar will cover the calibration and use of analytical weighing instruments (balances and laboratory/ben

items.title: 2066 Fundamentals of Metrology
items.content_text: An official website of the United States government

items.title: Apollo 18-Month Update – Apollo Research
items.content_text: Apollo Research is now 18 months old. You can read our latest update here.

items.title: Apollo Is Adopting Inspect – Apollo Research
items.content_text: Apollo is adopting Inspect as its evals framework. We will contribute features and potentially example agent evals to Inspect and look forward to work with the Inspect community

items.title: The First Year Of Apollo Research – Apollo Research
items.content_text: A summary of what we have achieved in our first year and what we plan to do in the future.

items.title: Theories of Change for AI Auditing – Apollo Research
items.content_text: This is a starter guide for model evaluations (evals). Our goal is to provide a general overview of what evals are, what skills are helpful for evaluators, potential career trajectories, and possible ways to start in the field of evals.

items.title: Security at Apollo Research – Apollo Research
items.content_text: A brief overview of Security at Apollo Research

items.title: Announcing Apollo Research – Apollo Research
items.content_text: A quick overview of our plans for the near future

items.title: Cheating behaviour in frontier model evaluations  | AISI Work
items.content_text: We find cheating behaviour in all of our capability evaluations, and outline the implications as models grow more capable.

items.title: How Far Behind the Frontier are Leading Open Weight Models on Cyber? | AISI Work
items.content_text: We evaluated the cyber capabilities of leading open and closed weight AI models, and found that recent open models GLM-5.2 and DeepSeek V4-Pro perform similarly to frontier closed models released 4 to 7 months before them – a narrower gap than the 6 to 10 months we measured through most of 2025.

items.title: Finding Cloud Misconfigurations with Frontier AI: A Case Study | AISI Work
items.content_text: A cybersecurity exercise from AISI’s engineering team, using frontier models to test our research platform for misconfigurations.

items.title: More compute, more capability: Why AI agent evaluations need to account for test-time compute | AISI Work
items.content_text: Standard evaluations cap how much compute AI agents can use. We show that raising those caps changes measured capability, the difficulty of tasks agents can solve, and how fast the frontier appears to move.

items.title: UK-Germany Joint Statement on advanced AI safety and security | AISI Work
items.content_text: A joint statement by the UK and Germany on collaborating to ensure advanced AI is developed safely and its risks are rigorously understood and managed.

items.title: Releasing AISI’s Engineering Playbook | AISI Work
items.content_text: Building on the momentum of the Inspect toolkit, we’re open-sourcing parts of the research stack behind AISI's evaluations.

items.title: RealityTest: Do AI systems disclose their identity when asked? | AISI Work
items.content_text: A new benchmark grounded in how real users actually probe AI identity during interactions – covering five languages, across text and speech.

items.title: Deepening our partnership with the Australian AI Safety Institute | AISI Work
items.content_text: An agreement between Institutes to collaborate on best practices in AI evaluation, and share research findings.

items.title: Will it become harder to oversee AI systems? | AISI Work
items.content_text: Our new report examines today’s AI oversight landscape, how robust it is to capability advances, and the pathways that could lead to its degradation.

items.title: How fast is autonomous AI cyber capability advancing? | AISI Work
items.content_text: The length of tasks frontier models can autonomously complete in our narrow cyber suite has been doubling every few months. This doubling rate has become faster over time, and recent models exceeded our previous trends.

items.title: Expenditure Horizon: Measuring Optimization Ability, with an Application to NanoGPT
items.content_text: We propose a measure of an AI agent’s optimization ability with an "expenditure horizon." We give an empirical illustration from the NanoGPT speedrun.

items.title: Frontier Risk Report (February to March 2026)
items.content_text: A pilot assessment of rogue deployment risk at frontier AI companies. Starting in February 2026, METR conducted a pilot exercise to assess misalignment risks from AI agents used inside frontier AI developers, with participation from Anthropic, Google, Meta, and OpenAI.

items.title: Measuring the Self-Reported Impact of Early-2026 AI on Technical Worker Productivity
items.content_text: A survey of 349 technical workers finds a median 1.4–2x self-reported change in value of work due to AI tools, expected to grow over time, though there are reasons to be skeptical of the magnitude.

items.title: Task Substitution and Uplift
items.content_text: We distinguish three measures of AI uplift -- on old tasks, on new tasks, and in value -- and show that task substitution can cause these to diverge substantially.

items.title: MirrorCode: Evidence that AI can already do some weeks-long coding tasks
items.content_text: Early results from MirrorCode benchmark with METR: AI agents can complete weeks-long coding tasks, including reimplementing a 16,000-line codebase.

items.title: We are Changing our Developer Productivity Experiment Design
items.content_text: Our second developer productivity study faces selection effects from wider AI adoption, prompting us to redesign our approach.

items.title: Time Horizon 1.1
items.content_text: We’re releasing a new version of our time horizon estimates (TH1.1), using more tasks and a new eval infrastructure.

items.title: Early work on monitorability evaluations
items.content_text: We show preliminary results on a prototype evaluation that tests monitors' ability to catch AI agents doing side tasks, and AI agents' ability to bypass this monitoring.

items.title: MALT: A Dataset of Natural and Prompted Behaviors That Threaten Eval Integrity
items.content_text: MALT (Manually-reviewed Agentic Labeled Transcripts) is a dataset of natural and prompted examples of behaviors that threaten evaluation integrity (like generalized reward hacking or sandbagging).

items.title: Forecasting the Impacts of AI R&D Acceleration: Results of a Pilot Study
items.content_text: AI agents are improving rapidly at autonomous software development and machine learning tasks, and, if recent trends hold, may match human researchers at challenging months-long research projects in under a decade. Some economic models predict that automation of AI research by AI agents could increase the pace of further progress dramatically, with many years of progress at the current rate being compressed into months. AI developers have identified this as a key capability to monitor and prepare for, since the national security implications and societal impacts of such rapid progress could be enormous.

items.title: Blog – Apollo Research
items.content_text: Apollo Research is focused on reducing risks from scheming frontier AI. We conduct fundamental research into the science of scheming and run pre-deployment evaluations of frontier AI systems. Our governance team provides global policymakers with expert technical guidance, and our product team builds AGI safety tools to monitor and secure frontier AI agents.
